generated_answer: Hirschsprung disease is a multifactorial, non-mendelian disorder, though simple Mendelian inheritance is rarely observed. It has a strong genetic basis with mutations in several genes contributing to risk, including *RET*, but incomplete penetrance suggests the involvement of genetic modifier loci. While Mendelian modes of inheritance have been described in syndromic cases, isolated Hirschsprung disease demonstrates complex inheritance patterns and can be influenced by common, low-penetrance variants.

Epidermal growth factor, amphiregulin, transforming growth factor alpha, neuregulin 2 beta, epigen, betacellulin, heparin-binding EGF-like growth factor, epiregulin, prolidase, and fibroblast growth factor receptor 4-induced amphiregulin all interact with the epidermal growth factor receptor as ligands.

Yes, papilin is a secreted extracellular matrix protein, as it is described as homologous to other secreted proteins and found in culture media. It is also stated to be a glycoprotein, indicating it is released from cells.

Yes, long non-coding RNAs can be spliced. One approach to predict them combines comparative genomics and machine learning based on detecting signatures of characteristic splice site evolution, assembling compatible sites into exon candidates, and predicting multi-exon transcripts. Additionally, the discovery approach relies on predicted splice sites to uncover both coding and non-coding exons.

Yes, activated human T cells express alternative mRNA transcripts encoding a secreted form of RANKL, and this transcript can be translated and secreted by Jurkat T cells in vitro. Additionally, synovial fibroblasts secrete factors that induce osteoclastogenesis, and osteoblasts secrete RANKL which impacts bone resorption.

No, metformin does not significantly alter levothyroxine absorption. One study examined the effect of metformin ingestion on levothyroxine absorption and found no significant differences in peak serum thyroxine concentrations or time to peak concentrations before and after metformin use, and only a trend toward reduced thyroxine AUC.

Several miRNAs—including miR-132, miR-26a, let-7b, miR-145, miR-21, miR-92, miR-93, miR-126, miR-29a, miR-155, miR-127, miR-99b, miR-200a, miR-100, miR-141, miR-200b, and miR-200c—could potentially serve as biomarkers for epithelial ovarian cancer, with miR-9 and miR-223 showing promise for recurrent ovarian cancer.

Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors are a mainstay of treatment for myasthenia gravis, providing temporary symptomatic relief of muscle weakness, and include treatments such as intranasal neostigmine, though large randomized trials have not been conducted on their use except for this small, inconclusive trial.

Yes, denosumab has been approved by the Food and Drug Administration since June 2010 as a treatment for postmenopausal osteoporosis in women at high risk for fracture, and also as a new treatment for postmenopausal osteoporosis generally. It was also approved for use in men, though only for postmenopausal osteoporosis currently.
The human genes encoding for dishevelled proteins are Dishevelled-1 (Dvl-1) and Dishevelled-2, and vertebrates also possess a multi-gene family including Dvl3.
Bazex' syndrome is a synonym of Acrokeratosis paraneoplastica.

Anti-arrhythmic drugs are classified into four classes according to Vaughan Williams, based on electrophysiological effects such as blockade of ion channels—sodium channels for class I and calcium channels for class IV, interaction with receptors for class II, and alteration of electrophysiological parameters like action potential duration for class III.

There are four different mammalian Notch receptors—Notch1, Notch2, Notch3, and a fourth unspecified receptor—that can be activated by five cell surface ligands and share similar structures despite having only partially overlapping functions.

Cellular senescence is characterized by arrested cell-cycle progression, the development of aberrant gene expression with proinflammatory behavior, slower proliferation, altered morphology, and can be evidenced by positive staining for senescence-associated β-Gal.
Orteronel was developed as a therapy for prostate cancer and may also be effective for hormone-dependent breast cancers and estrogen-dependent diseases.

Yes, trastuzumab (Herceptin) shows potential, though limited as a single agent, in treating prostate cancer. It targets the HER-2 receptor, which is implicated in prostate cancer development, and a novel antibody, Erb-hcAb, demonstrated more potent antitumor effects than Herceptin in some cell lines. However, a phase II trial showed poor efficacy as a single agent for hormone-refractory prostate cancer, though combination approaches with cytotoxic agents might be more effective.
The Yamanaka factors are Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, and c-Myc, which are transcription factors capable of inducing pluripotency in somatic cells and critically regulating developmental signaling networks.

The Chromosome-Centric Human Proteome Project aims to map and annotate all proteins encoded by genes on each human chromosome. It focuses on finding evidence of expression for all proteins on each chromosome, prioritizing those with post-translational modifications and those found in low abundance. This project builds a catalog chromosome-by-chromosome, particularly concentrating on “missing proteins” that currently lack evidence from mass spectrometry or antibody detection.

Pannexin1 traffics to the plasma membrane in mammals and forms large-pore voltage-gated membrane channels involved in calcium and ATP signaling, while in zebrafish it is expressed in the inner and outer retinae and exhibits non-overlapping protein localization in various brain areas.

POLG mutations have been associated with a variety of mitochondrial diseases including Alpers syndrome, progressive external ophthalmoplegia (both autosomal dominant and recessive forms), ataxia-neuropathy syndromes, Myocerebrohepatopathy Spectrum disorders, Myoclonus Epilepsy Myopathy Sensory Ataxia, Parkinsonism, premature menopause, and a condition mimicking mitochondrial neurogastrointestinal encephalomyopathy.

Ivabradine improves left ventricular function in heart failure and reduces remodeling following myocardial infarction. In rat models, ivabradine, similar to metoprolol, partially prevented deterioration of left ventricular ejection fraction and reduced wall stress post-MI. Additionally, ivabradine prevented the depression of sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase activity and increased cardiac angiogenesis, either early or late after myocardial infarction.
Wilson's disease exhibits an autosomal recessive mode of inheritance, as confirmed by genetic analyses of families and segregation ratios.

Yes, transcription and splicing are connected and frequently occur together, even influencing each other reciprocally. Evidence suggests both processes are coordinated, functionally coupled, and can be influenced by factors like elongation rates and the presence of proteins with dual activities in both processes. Furthermore, chromatin structure, which regulates transcription, may also have a role in splicing.
Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD) is characterized by an autosomal dominant mode of inheritance.

Yes, Alu hypomethylation is associated with breast cancer, specifically correlating with negative estrogen receptor (ER) status in invasive breast cancer and being prominent in the HER2 enriched subtype. It also tends to be associated with poor disease-free survival in patients.

The ryanodine receptor quaternary complex consists of calsequestrin, triadin, and junctin, and these proteins interact to anchor calsequestrin to the ryanodine receptor, potentially requiring normal operation of calcium release.
Hydrophilic-interaction chromatography is a valuable complementary approach to reversed-phase liquid chromatography and a widely used technique for small polar molecule analysis.

TRH improves myocardial contractility, as demonstrated by increases in parameters like dp/dtmax, Vpm, and Vmax. It potentiates the inotropic effects of isoprenaline and dopamine on the left atrium, and can directly exert a positive inotropic effect on isolated rat hearts. This effect is linked to the adrenergic system, specifically stimulating epinephrine secretion and sensitizing beta-receptors, and may function as an autocrine regulator within the heart itself.

Yes, the complete genome of several bacteria within the genus Arthrobacter is available. Specifically, the complete genome sequence of Arthrobacter phenanthrenivorans type strain (Sphe3), Arthrobacter sp. Rue61a, and Arthrobacter sp. ZXY-2 have been described, along with details regarding their chromosome and plasmid structures.

Bromodomain proteins generally possess a left-handed, four-helix bundle topology, though some, like that of P/CAF, exhibit an unusual up-and-down arrangement, and BRG1 contains a small beta-sheet in the ZA loop region in addition to an all-alpha-helical fold.
Esophageal achalasia, ileocolonic lymphoid nodular hyperplasia, and mucosal inflammation have been endoscopically diagnosed in children with autism.

Renal sympathetic denervation results in significant and sustained reductions in blood pressure, and also reduces heart rate and sympathetic nerve activity. The procedure appears to be safe, with a minimal complication rate, and these effects are observed for at least three years after treatment without evidence of re-innervation. Beyond resistant hypertension, renal denervation shows potential benefits for conditions associated with enhanced sympathetic activity, such as heart failure, chronic kidney disease, and metabolic syndrome.
c-Jun N-terminal kinase (Jnk) phosphorylates the transcription factor c-Jun at the activating sites Ser-63 and Ser-73.

TAILS stands for Terminal Amine Isotopic Labeling of Substrates and is a combined N-terminomics and protease substrate discovery degradomics platform for the simultaneous quantitative and global analysis of the N-terminome and proteolysis.

Yes, mutations in AKT1 occur in meningiomas. Several sources indicate recurrent AKT1 mutations are found in meningiomas, specifically the E17K mutation, and these mutations can be detected via SFRP1 immunohistochemistry. These mutations are seen exclusively in meningiomas and are associated with specific subtypes and locations of the tumors. Additionally, alterations in AKT1 are identified alongside mutations in other genes like TRAF7 and KLF4 within meningiomas.

Lacosamide shows efficacy in treating partial-onset and generalized tonic-clonic epilepsies, sound-induced seizures, maximal electroshock test-induced seizures, the rat hippocampal kindling model of partial seizures, the 6Hz model of psychomotor seizures, and NMDA-induced seizures, and it is used in ongoing clinical trials for these indications as well as potentially optimizing treatment of epilepsy alongside carbamazepine, clobazam, ethosuximide, gabapentin, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, oxcarbazepine, phenobarbital, phenytoin, primidone, topiramate, and zonisamide.
The EWS-FLI1 fusion protein, created by the t(11;22)(q24;q12) chromosomal translocation, is involved in the development of Ewing sarcoma and functions as an aberrant transcriptional activator.

Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome is characterized by the triad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute renal failure, sometimes manifesting as microangiopathy alongside these conditions.

Yes, one study measured fasting gut hormones and found they remained unchanged during bed rest, while another indicates exercise increases the release of gut hormones like gastrin, CCK, and PP immediately after exercise. Additionally, exercise can modify the gut microbiota, which is linked to colorectal cancer and characterized by dysbiosis including changes in short chain fatty acid-producing bacteria.

Depleting km23-1 reduces TGFβ1 mRNA expression, DNA binding, and transcriptional activation, inhibits TGFβ-mediated activation of ERK and JNK, phosphorylation of c-Jun, and transactivation of the c-Jun promoter, decreases RhoA activation and cell migration, inhibits constitutive ERK activation and downstream effector functions like phosphorylation of Elk-1 and c-Fos-DNA binding, reduces TGFβ1 secretion and expression of Ezrin, lowers intracellular and nuclear levels of phosphorylated Smad2, causes proteasomal degradation of activated Smad2, and ultimately suppresses tumor formation and invasion.
The MR CLEAN study investigated treatment for acute ischemic stroke caused by a proximal intracranial occlusion of the anterior circulation.

Nanog, Pou5f1, and SoxB1 activate zygotic gene expression during the maternal-to-zygotic transition in zebrafish, and these factors also regulate the activation of miR-430 expression to induce clearance of the maternal program.

Yes, women with endometriosis are more likely to receive a diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome. Data show women with endometriosis were 3.5 times more likely to have been diagnosed with irritable bowel syndrome compared to controls, and those with a history of irritable bowel syndrome were twice as likely to develop endometriosis.

The EORTC QLQ-INFO25 questionnaire evaluates the information received by cancer patients. It measures a patient’s perception of information, including details about their disease, medical tests, treatment, and other services. This tool is used to assess informational needs and disclosure, and has been validated across multiple cultures and countries.

Yes, BNP rises significantly after strenuous endurance exercise in athletes, and NT-proBNP also increases in a major part of healthy athletes after prolonged strenuous exercise. Increases in NT-proBNP were observed in all events examined, and were not related to the exercise-induced immune response.

Available data suggest an association between the use of hormone replacement therapy and increased meningioma risk, though this is not definitive. One study showed the risk of meningioma was about twice as high in users of postmenopausal hormone replacement therapy as in non-users, and it should be discontinued upon diagnosis. However, other studies found only a weak association or no significant association between meningioma risk and hormone replacement therapy use. Further evaluation is needed, specifically stratifying by hormone composition, duration of use, age at use, and tumor receptor subtype.

Yes, several studies demonstrate the potential value and acceptability of web-based self-management interventions for chronic pain. These interventions include informational websites, smartphone-based diaries with therapist feedback, and interactive web-based programs designed to enhance self-management skills and monitor symptoms. Some programs also deliver individualized guidance based on self-monitoring data.

Yes, considerable phenotypic overlap between the two syndromes is evident, and they are closely related conditions. Both involve overgrowth and share similar characteristics, making diagnosis difficult, though they have distinctive facial appearances. NSD1 mutations are found in Sotos syndrome and a significant number of Weaver syndrome cases.
Evolocumab targets proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9).

Yes, a large fraction of non-exonic ultraconserved elements are transcribed across developmental stages, and many are transcribed in multiple tissues. Some transcripts might even encode nuclear transcripts, and several elements are found overlapping transcribed portions of genes.
The methyl donor of DNA (cytosine-5)-methyltransferases is S-adenosylmethionine (SAM).

Yes, peripheral neuroepithelioma and Ewing’s sarcoma are closely related tumors, potentially representing phenotypic variations of the same peripheral primitive neuroectodermal tumor. They share similar neuroectodermal features and genetic characteristics, including the t(11;22) translocation, making them difficult to distinguish. Both are also identified by expression of the glycoprotein p30/32MIC2.
Sonidegib inhibits the hedgehog signaling pathway by targeting smoothened, a key component and regulator of this pathway.
Arrest of S-phase progression is impaired in Fanconi anemia cells, and the disorder also features incomplete DNA replication and arrest in late S phase as well as an inefficient S-phase checkpoint.

R-loops can form in genomic DNA regions having a tract of G bases in the non-template strand, through triplet microsatellite DNA sequences, and at repeat tracts including (CTG)n, (CAG)n, (CGG)n, (CCG)n, and (GAA)n, as well as over G-rich terminator elements and CpG island promoters.
Mutations in the aryl hydrocarbon receptor-interacting protein gene are implicated in familial isolated pituitary adenoma.

Mutations including c.363dupG (or p.Gln122AlafsX30), A8V, C84Y, E134D, and D145E in the troponin C gene have been found to cause hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, with D145E drastically altering calcium binding and increasing calcium sensitivity of force development.

Acupuncture has been explored as a treatment for smoking cessation, often focusing on specific points on the ear. Success rates vary considerably across studies, ranging from 0.6% to 53.8%, with one-year success rates reaching 41.1% in some cases. Combining acupuncture with education appears to enhance its effect, particularly for individuals with a greater smoking history, though maintaining long-term differences can be challenging.

Constitutive heterochromatin is characterized by hypoacetylation of histones, methylation of lysine 9 on histone H3, methylation of lysine 20 on histone H4, and trimethylation of lysine 9 on histone H3.
GV1001 vaccine targets human telomerase reverse transcriptase, a reverse-transcriptase-subunit of telomerase.
Mdm2 is the E3 ubiquitin ligase that ubiquitinates p53 to target it for proteasomal degradation.

Yes, the HGF/c-Met axis induces activation of the PI3K/Akt pathway, and Cyr61 is a downstream effector of HGF/c-Met pathway activation. Additionally, inhibiting c-Met activity suppresses the PI3K-Akt pathway.

Yes, pregnancy constitutes a high-risk condition for complications from influenza infection, including H1N1, and is associated with increased morbidity and mortality. It particularly increases risk during the third trimester, and early intervention with antiviral medications is warranted if infection is suspected.

No, some long non-coding RNAs are derived from lost protein-coding genes and exhibit broader expression domains, setting them apart from other lncRNAs, while others show patterns of codon substitution typical of non-coding transcripts and significant sequence conservation relative to their flanking regions – regions that often lack homology. Additionally, conserved lncRNAs exhibit stable secondary structure motifs, suggesting a different mode of conservation than protein-coding genes.
Mutations in the NKX2-1 gene cause brain-lung-thyroid syndrome, leading to neurological, pulmonary, and thyroid hormone impairment.

Clathrin is a protein that assembles with others into structures like clathrin-coated pits, which mediate vesicle formation and cell-surface protein internalization. It is involved in clathrin-mediated endocytosis, a major pathway for macromolecules to enter eukaryotic cells. Clathrin also participates in the formation of tubulobulbar complexes and can be phosphorylated, influencing actin recruitment at sites of adhesion and internalization. Additionally, clathrin requires uncoating factors to function in cellular trafficking processes.

PRKAR1A knockdown inhibits cholangiocarcinoma cell growth and enhances apoptosis. Silencing PRKAR1A expression leads to growth inhibition and apoptosis of CCA cells, accompanied by a decrease in signaling from mitogen-activated protein kinases, PI3K/Akt, JAK/STAT and Wnt/β-catenin pathways. Additionally, PRKAR1A suppression increases sensitivity to certain chemotherapeutic drugs and enhances the apoptotic effects of sorafenib or 5-fluorouracil when combined with PRKAR1A knockdown. In fibrolamellar carcinoma, loss of PRKAR1A protein expression and mutations in the gene are observed, contributing to protein kinase A activation.

Yes, transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation is widely used in pain management and has demonstrated hypoalgesic effects, particularly at a frequency of 4 Hz, in reducing experimentally induced ischaemic pain. It is also noted that its effectiveness depends on appropriate targeting of the stimulation.

Yes, several algorithms exist for enhancer identification from chromatin state. RFECS is a random-forest based algorithm, DELTA utilizes an AdaBoost algorithm and shape features, and ChromaGenSVM employs support vector machines combined with genetic algorithm optimization for this purpose.
Imetelstat targets the enzyme telomerase, which is reactivated in tumor cells and critical for cancer cell immortality.
Dupilumab inhibits interleukins 4 and 13, and it blocks the signal transduction of both by targeting the α subunit of the interleukin-4 receptor.

FGFR2, FGFR3, FGFR1, TWIST1, EFNB1, RAB23, and BMP2 genes are more commonly related to craniosynostosis, with mutations in the FGFR family being particularly associated with Apert, Pfeiffer, Crouzon, and Antley-Bixler syndromes, and mutations in FGFR3 being the most common genetic cause of Muenke syndrome.

Yes, transcribed ultraconserved regions are involved in cancer. They are altered in adult chronic lymphocytic leukemias, carcinomas, pediatric neuroblastomas, prostate cancer, gastric cancer, and breast cancer, and may play a role in tumorigenesis and cancer development. Aberrant expression profiles can differentiate human cancer types, and they are associated with poor outcomes in some patients.

Palbociclib can be used in patients with hormone receptor-positive, human epidermal growth factor receptor 2-negative advanced or metastatic breast cancer, either in combination with letrozole or fulvestrant, and also in combination with aromatase inhibitors for postmenopausal women.

Yes, congenital sensorineural deafness is a characteristic of Pendred syndrome, appearing in association with familial goiter and, in many cases, enlargement of the vestibular aqueduct. Several reports detail patients with Pendred syndrome presenting with congenital profound or severe sensorineural deafness.

Side effects of SGLT2 inhibitors include repeated urinary tract and genital infections, increased hematocrit, decreased blood pressure, mild urinal and genital infections, and potentially limited use based on kidney function requiring an estimated glomerular filtration rate greater than 60 mL/min/1.73.

Yes, CD56 expression correlates with progression-free survival in Ewing sarcoma, with high expression indicating a less favorable prognosis, particularly in localized nonpelvic disease. Additionally, flow cytometry immunophenotyping detecting CD56 expression can be useful in undifferentiated small, round blue cell tumors.

Filter-aided sample preparation is used to digest proteomic samples solubilized in high concentrations of SDS, remove OCT contaminating species from samples, and clean up samples following detergent lysis for enhanced proteome characterization.

Reduced dosage of ERF, which is directly bound by ERK1/2, causes complex craniosynostosis in both humans and mice. ERK1/2 are key proteins that mediate signaling downstream of RAS, and their phosphorylation leads to nuclear uptake and modulation of targets. This work identifies ERF as a regulator of osteogenic stimulation by RAS-ERK signaling, potentially by competing with activating ETS factors. Therefore, ERK1/2 signaling appears to be involved in the regulation of osteogenesis and the development of craniosynostosis.

Yes, several urine biomarkers exist for chronic kidney disease. Neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL), kidney injury molecule-1 (KIM-1), and podocin have garnered attention as potential markers for detecting kidney damage earlier than traditional methods like serum creatinine and proteinuria. Additionally, databases integrating urine proteomes are being utilized to identify new biomarker candidates for both the development and progression of chronic kidney disease.

Accelerometers are used to measure maximum voluntary contraction, physical activity in daily life, whole-body acceleration, and activity levels sampled every minute to assess the relationship between pain and activity.

JASPAR has undergone eight major releases, beginning with its initial release and followed by updates in 2008, 2010, a new generation release, 2014, and culminating in the 2020 release which expanded the CORE collection and introduced new features like unvalidated profiles and a Q&A forum.

IFAP syndrome is characterized by ichthyosis follicularis, absence of hair, photophobia, hairless patches on the scalp, ichthyosiform skin changes, hypotrichosis, hyperkeratosis, nail dystrophy, brown scaly plaques, pachyonychia, palmoplantar and periorificial keratoderma, and potentially hyperextensibility of the fingers.
The cytochrome P450 2C19 gene and the P2Y12 gene are required for the efficient function of clopidogrel.

Yes, valproic acid shows potential benefit in glioblastoma treatment. Several studies indicate it may improve survival, particularly when administered within two weeks of diagnosis or concurrently with radiation therapy and temozolomide. It also appears to reduce hair loss associated with treatment and may have radiosensitizing effects on gliomas while protecting normal brain tissue.
Transcription factor EB is the only known transcription factor that is a master regulator of lysosomal biogenesis.

Bacitracin, fosfomycin, ampicillin, D-cycloserine, ramoplanin, and vancomycin target peptidoglycan biosynthesis, as do antibiotics generally considered for potential as vaccine candidates and antimicrobial therapies against Streptococcus pneumoniae.

No, levothyroxine sodium does not appear to cause insomnia. A study examined the efficacy of levothyroxine and liothyronine compared to levothyroxine alone and found a small but significant *reduction* in anxiety/insomnia in the combined treatment group, suggesting levothyroxine itself is not a cause of insomnia.

Yes, fatigue is a commonly reported side effect in patients with glioblastoma relapse treated with irinotecan-bevacizumab, and a progressive increase in physical fatigue has been demonstrated in these patients. Additionally, cancer-related fatigue is described as a disabling and distressing symptom highly prevalent across the cancer continuum, including during and after cancer treatment.
Tay syndrome commonly presents with congenital ichthyosis and brittle hair, often accompanied by physical and mental retardation.

B cells, T cells, and synovial fluid cells are known to be driving rheumatoid arthritis, with genetic risk factors identified near genes such as IL6ST, SPRED2, RBPJ, CCR6, IRF5, and PXK, and the disease can also be influenced by interactions between medications like methotrexate and nitrofurantoin.

Elevated neuroticism is associated with an increased risk for Alzheimer’s disease. This association has been observed in multiple studies, predicting both earlier age of onset and increased risk over extended follow-up periods. In some instances, the link between neuroticism and Alzheimer’s disease diminished when accounting for long-standing distress, and a combination of high neuroticism and low extraversion showed the highest risk.

Everolimus interacts with mTOR and inhibits the Akt-mTOR-p70S6K pathway, ultimately blocking cell proliferation. It functions as an inhibitor of the mammalian target of rapamycin, offering a different mode of action and side effect profile compared to calcineurin inhibitors. As an mTOR inhibitor, it impacts mRNA translation, ribosome biogenesis, cell cycle progression, and growth, and is used as an anticancer agent in treatments for renal cell carcinoma and mantle cell lymphoma.

Yes, SNPs in splicing sites, identified as splicing QTLs (sQTLs), have been found to be associated with highLDLhighTG, and some sQTLs fall within genome-wide association study SNPs, suggesting a link to disease. Additionally, the methodology for multivariate phenotype-based genome-wide association studies can identify genetic risk factors accounting for pleiotropic effects when multivariate phenotypes have a common etiological pathway.
Reverse transcriptase-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) is used for the detection of EWS-FLI1 fusion transcripts.

Yes, genome-wide studies reveal that CTCF globally co-localizes with cohesin, and multiple investigations have found substantial overlap between their binding sites. Furthermore, the interaction between CTCF and cohesin is essential for insulation activity and large-scale chromatin organization.

The Bimolecular Fluorescence Complementation (BiFC) assay is applied in Drosophila embryos to visualize and study protein interactions in a physiologically relevant developing context, analyze dynamic protein interactions, and identify protein partners, particularly of Hox proteins.

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is a genetic disease of the heart and the most common inherited cardiovascular disorder. It is characterized by myocyte hypertrophy resulting in thickening of the ventricular wall, myocyte disarray, and fibrosis. The condition involves left ventricular hypertrophy and can range from being asymptomatic to causing sudden cardiac death.

Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome is a genetic disorder caused by mutations in the CREBBP and EP300 genes, or potentially by other as-yet-undetectable mutations, and is characterized by aberrant chromatin regulation.

The viral KP4 protein is a toxin secreted by fungi that kills sensitive fungal strains and inhibits growth of Fusarium and plant roots by inhibiting calcium uptake. It functions by creating pores in cell membranes and disrupting ion fluxes, and it specifically targets smuts and bunts in wheat. KP4 can be used to increase fungal resistance in plants, like wheat and tobacco, and has a low risk to humans and the environment due to its specificity. It is a single polypeptide subunit with a molecular weight of 11.1 kDa and is predominantly comprised of beta-strands.
The AIRE gene promotes the self-renewal of embryonic stem cells by regulating the expression of pluripotent factors like Lin28 and differentiation-associated genes.

PAR-CLIP relies on incorporating photoreactive ribonucleoside analogs, such as 4-thiouridine and 6-thioguanosine, into nascent RNA transcripts within living cells. Upon UV light irradiation, these analogs efficiently crosslink to interacting RNA-binding proteins. Following immunoprecipitation and sequencing, the precise crosslinking position can be identified, allowing for transcriptome-wide mapping of RBP binding sites. This method is used to identify miRNA-target interactions and determine posttranscriptional gene regulation.

Potassium perchlorate, carbimazole, propylthiouracil, lithium bicarbonate, lithium carbonate, and steroids are utilized to treat amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis, sometimes in combination with one another or alongside continued amiodarone treatment.

Spastic diplegia is diagnosed by identifying specific leg movements in infants—simultaneous flexion and extension of the hips and knees—and the absence of other movements like hip flexion combined with knee extension. Physicians can utilize a diagnostic algorithm to assist in making the correct diagnosis, as many other disorders can mimic spastic diplegia. Gait analysis and physical examinations assessing muscle tone and motor control can also reveal distinctive patterns associated with the condition, particularly in children with HIV encephalopathy.

Neurofibromatosis type 1 is caused by mutations in the NF1 gene, a megagene located on the long arm of the 17th chromosome, which encodes the protein neurofibromin that functions as a tumor-suppressor.
GPX1 and GPX4 are human selenoproteins that contain SECIS elements and are proportional to steady-state mRNA levels.
r3Cseq is an R/Bioconductor package designed to perform analysis of long-range genomic interactions from chromosome conformation capture and next-generation sequencing data.

Four clinical trials involving products that include neonates are mentioned, and domperidone is cited as being used off-label in children older than 12 years, though its efficacy in infants is unknown.

Yes, stress granules are involved in the pathogenesis of Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis. Aberrant forms of these structures have been implicated in the disease, and proteins associated with ALS, including TDP-43 and FUS, localize to them. Furthermore, dysfunction induced by prolonged stress granule formation may contribute directly to the disease, and modulating stress granules can rescue toxicity in disease models. Mutant FUS proteins also assemble into stress granules, potentially linking FUS mutations to altered motor neuron homeostasis.

Yes, TGF-beta appears to play a role in cardiac regeneration after myocardial infarction. Pre-programming CD117+ cells with TGF-beta effectively assisted myocardial regeneration and induced therapeutic angiogenesis, contributing to functional cardiac regeneration. Additionally, TGF-beta receptor III is involved in regulating the TGF-β pathway and can influence collagen production in cardiac fibroblasts following myocardial infarction.

Yes, there is a genetic component to happiness. Studies estimate that genetic factors account for 22-50% of the variance in human happiness, and specific genes like MAOA and those in the cannabinoid receptor gene are associated with happiness levels. Furthermore, research suggests the same genes can influence both happiness and environmental factors contributing to well-being.  Genome-wide linkage studies have also identified chromosomal regions potentially contributing to happiness heritability.
Opicapone inhibits catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) enzyme activity.

Soluble lysosomal proteins can be purified using affinity chromatography on an immobilized mannose-6-phosphate (M6P) receptor. This method relies on the characteristic sugar, M6P, found on these proteins, allowing for easy purification. Several studies have successfully used this approach with immobilized M6P receptors to isolate and identify lysosomal proteins from various sources, including cells in culture, urine, and brain tissue.

NKX2.2, NR0B1, BCL11B, and SPRY1 are genes thought to be regulated by EWS/FLI, alongside many others identified through transcriptional profiling and meta-analysis revealing a core EWS-FLI gene expression signature involved in tumorigenesis.

Archaeal genomes can contain either a single origin of replication, as previously known, or multiple origins, with some species like *Sulfolobus* having three functional origins and others like *Aeropyrum pernix* having two.

Mutations in the CYLD gene cause Brooke-Spiegler syndrome, familial cylindromatosis, familial trichoepithelioma, and can contribute to the aggressiveness of epidermal squamous cell carcinomas, as well as, rarely, classical Hodgkin lymphoma.

Mutations of the SMN1 gene, deletions or mutations of the SMN1 gene, and distortions of SMN1 function are responsible for Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA), though a mutation in the VRK1 gene can cause a rare variant of the disease.

Common symptoms of Cushing’s syndrome include weight gain, growth retardation, hirsutism, obesity, striae, acne, hypertension, fragile skin, easy bruising, reddish purple striae, facial plethora, and hair loss, while some cases may also present with androgen excess.
TBC1D7 is the third subunit of the TSC1-TSC2 complex upstream of mTORC1.
KN-93 inhibits Ca2+/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II (CaM kinase II), as well as multifunctional Ca++/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase.

Chk2 splice variants can have a dominant-negative effect on wild-type Chk2 kinase activity.  Specifically, these variants impair the activity of wild-type Chk2 through heterodimerization, regardless of whether they possess intrinsic kinase activity themselves. Alternative splicing may represent a novel mechanism for repressing the function of wild-type Chk2.  It was demonstrated that expressing a dominant-negative Chk2 blocked cell cycle arrest.

The PMP22 gene is mutated in CMT1A through duplication or, less commonly, point mutations and triplication, and dynamin 2 mutations lead to a related neuropathy type B, though this is distinct from CMT1A.

Enteroviruses, human parechovirus type 3, and H1N1 influenza are known to cause myocarditis, with coxsackievirus A6 and HPeV3 being the most commonly reported types during a surveillance period from 2009-2013.

KRIT1, MGC4607, CCM1, CCM2, and CCM3 genes have been associated with cerebral cavernous malformation, as well as PDCD10 mutations which contribute to an aggressive form of the disease, and sterile 20-like kinases such as STK24 and STK25 play a role in the condition’s development.

Yes, DITPA is a thyroid hormone analog currently in phase II clinical trials and has been used in experimental studies involving hamsters and mice, as well as in investigations applying for FDA approval to study its use in patients with heart failure. It has demonstrated beneficial effects on cardiac function, chamber remodeling, and infarct size in these studies.

Tarlov cysts are sacral perineural cysts, which are nerve root cysts most often found in the sacral region. They arise between the covering layer of the perineurium and the endoneurium near the dorsal root ganglion and are commonly detected incidentally on MRI. While often asymptomatic, these cysts can cause sacral or lumbar pain syndromes, sciatica, or even more rarely, cauda equina syndrome, and may increase in size over time. Treatment options include laminectomy and excision or obliteration of the cyst, though surgical intervention is debated for symptomatic cases.

'Vildagliptin', 'sitagliptin', 'saxagliptin', 'linagliptin', and 'alogliptin' are dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitors, also known as gliptins, used in the pharmacologic management of type 2 diabetes, while 'dutogliptin' is another DPP-4 inhibitor whose studies have been terminated.

The mutational status of immunoglobulin heavy variable (IGHV) genes, which defines mutated CLL (M-CLL) and unmutated CLL (U-CLL), is the most important prognostic marker in chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

No. The provided texts mention several items cleared or authorized by the United States Food and Drug Administration – HairMax LaserComb®, Paxlovid, JYNNEOS vaccine, and ustekinumab – but MammaPrint is not among them.

Yes, amantadine hydrochloride may promote functional recovery in patients in a vegetative or minimally conscious state after traumatic brain injury, as preliminary studies have suggested its benefit and a trial demonstrated faster recovery rates compared to placebo.

MMP proteins require N-glycosylation and interaction with LMAN1, a lectin-carrier protein, for efficient secretion and function in shaping the extracellular matrix, and selective inhibition of MMP-2 may be pursued for drug design.

Hyperosmia is a condition where individuals rate their sense of smell as far better than average and may experience primarily negative odor-related experiences, increased sensitivity to specific odors, and inconvenience caused by their sense of smell.
The human genome contains thousands of long non-coding RNAs, with one annotation identifying 3019 expressed in various human cell lines and tissue.
Clostridium botulinum is the bacterium responsible for botulism, originally associated with “sausage poisoning” and named after the Latin word for sausage, botulus.
Spermidine alleviates α-synuclein-induced neurodegeneration and toxicity by inducing autophagy, though it also promotes the early stages of α-synuclein misfolding and aggregation.

4H syndrome presents with hypomyelination, hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, hypodontia, ataxia, delayed tooth eruption, and potentially growth hormone deficiency, undescended testes, cerebellar ataxia, delayed cognitive development, and a thin corpus callosum as revealed by MRI.

The extracellular core matrisome comprises approximately 300 proteins in mammals. These proteins, along with ECM-modifying enzymes and associated proteins, cooperate to assemble and remodel extracellular matrices. The matrisome influences cell survival, proliferation, differentiation, shape, polarity, and motility, and plays a key role in the evolution of multicellularity and tissue structures.

Yes, GAGA factor is implicated in the formation of nucleosome-free regions of chromatin and interacts with sequences within these regions, as demonstrated by its role in the iab-7 Polycomb response element and its ability to function as a silencer. Additionally, GAGA factor interactions within nucleosome-free regions do not require ATP.

There are four monophyletic plant cytosine-5 DNA methyltransferase families, and a new potential methyltransferase gene family was also isolated, with members including MET1, MET2a, MET2b, and MET3, alongside DRM2 and DRM3 as well as others identified through sequence homology forming a small multigene family.

CENPA is a centromere-specific histone H3-like protein that localizes to the centromeric region of eukaryotic chromosomes and is incorporated into unique nucleosomes that provide the base for kinetochore formation.

The chromodomain is present in a wide range of chromatic and regulatory proteins involved in chromatin remodeling, as well as in proteins such as MRG15, Cbx3, and HP1, and also in proteins homologous to Drosophila HP1 and Cbx2, and lamin B receptor-associated proteins.

Genomicus is a database and an online tool for comparative genomic visualization, currently supporting over 150 eukaryote genomes and four different phyla—Vertebrate, Fungi, Metazoan, and Plants. It allows exploration of gene organization within and between genomes, as well as temporal relationships related to gene and genome evolution. The tool also provides access to reconstructed ancestral gene organization and conserved non-coding elements, facilitating evolutionary and comparative genomics studies.

No, while amiodarone is considered a class III anti-arrhythmic agent, it also possesses class I, II, and IV actions, indicating it is not solely a class I drug. Additionally, class I agents are generally excluded when treating arrhythmias in cardiogenic shock due to abnormal cardiac structure and function.

Myocardial scar demonstrated by late gadolinium enhancement is a good independent predictor of cardiac mortality and has incremental value for risk stratification and management in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and its presence is related to a higher frequency of atrial fibrillation and larger left atrial size, reinforcing its usefulness in assessment.

COUGER is a classification-based framework designed to identify protein co-factors that provide specificity to paralogous transcription factors. It takes genomic regions bound by these factors as input and identifies a set of co-factors that best distinguish between the sequences. This tool can be used as a stand-alone application or accessed through a web interface and presents identified co-factors with supporting information for user exploration.

Effective vaccines against Tick-borne Encephalitis exist, and while there is currently no specific treatment for infection, mexidol can be used as an add-on to antiviral treatment to improve outcomes and reduce residual signs, and piracetam has also been used though less effectively, while research is ongoing to understand host immune responses and identify new therapeutic targets like interferon.

Yes, SLC22A3 is expressed throughout the brain and is highly represented as OCT3, a subtype of organic cation transporters found in regions regulating fluid exchange and sensing changes in blood osmolarity. It is also expressed in aminergic pathways, specifically dopaminergic neurons and other brain structures.

Yes, TIEG1 has been associated with apoptosis. It plays a critical role in the apoptotic cascade, mimics the effects of TGF-beta in inducing apoptosis, and can induce apoptosis in HL-60 cells and oligodendroglial cells, as well as influence the ability of cells to undergo apoptosis. Additionally, the absence of TIEG1 can affect apoptosis rates in osteoclasts.
Opa1 is localized to the mitochondrial intermembrane space, with some isoforms associating with the outer and inner mitochondrial membranes.

Topiramate is a drug associated with burning mouth syndrome, and treatment may involve discontinuing the medication to resolve symptoms, while other approaches focus on symptomatic treatment and addressing potential psychological factors like depression and anxiety.

Yes, PTEN is involved in follicular thyroid carcinoma. Germline mutations in PTEN have been found in patients with follicular thyroid carcinoma, particularly those with macrocephaly, and loss of heterozygosity in the PTEN gene is observed in a portion of sporadic follicular carcinomas. Additionally, PTEN’s role is suggested in the pathogenesis of follicular thyroid tumors, and it is commonly affected in Cowden syndrome, which increases the risk of follicular thyroid cancer.
GBshape is a genome browser database for DNA shape annotations, freely available at http://rohslab.cmb.usc.edu/GBshape/.

Ceftriaxone is being investigated as a potential therapeutic agent for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis due to its ability to increase excitatory aminoacid transporter 2 (EAAT2) activity and glutamate transporter 1 (GLT1) expression. A multi-stage clinical trial assessed the safety and efficacy of ceftriaxone for the disease, and pharmacokinetic analysis supported planning a Phase 3 trial. However, in some patients with both Lyme disease and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, ceftriaxone treatment did not result in clinical improvement, and one case of motor neuron disease recovery occurred alongside ceftriaxone and anti-Babesia therapy.

Oncotype DX is used to predict which patients with ER-positive node-negative disease will benefit from chemotherapy, and the TAILORx trial is determining the benefit of chemotherapy for patients with mid-range recurrence scores from this assay. Patients enrolled on the TAILORx trial utilized Oncotype DX testing, initially on a trial basis and later for all eligible patients per national guidelines. Classification and regression tree analysis correctly classified cases into both Oncotype DX and TAILORx risk categories, suggesting a relationship in how patients are stratified for treatment decisions between the two.

Yes, strenuous physical activity can transiently change thyroid hormones, though these changes are typically minor and reflect energy balance during exercise. It may also be accompanied by a delay in skeletal maturation and potentially negative effects on growth, particularly during childhood or adolescence. Additionally, strenuous exercise in young female athletes can be associated with low T3 levels.

RNA sponges sequester cellular noncoding RNA molecules and can control endogenous small RNAs. They function by competing with other RNAs, specifically acting as microRNA sponges to regulate downstream gene transcription. Certain RNA sponges, like circular RNAs, can also act as RNA-binding protein sponges, influencing various biological processes including cancer development and progression. These sponges have potential applications in diagnosis, prognosis, and clinical therapy.

Yes, the MAOA gene’s promoter region is subject to methylation, and this methylation status can vary between individuals and is associated with differences in brain activity of the MAO A enzyme and, in some cases, gene expression. Studies have quantified DNA methylation in the MAOA promoter region using techniques like bisulfite pyrosequencing and bisulphite conversion followed by sequencing, demonstrating interindividual differences in methylation patterns and levels.

The PLN -42 C>G mutation has been associated with apical hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, while a deletion of arginine 14 in phospholamban results in late-onset, mild dilated cardiomyopathy, and no mutations were detected in the dilated cardiomyopathy group when screening for mutations in the human muscle LIM protein gene.

Antioxidant supplements investigated in athletes include those found in dietary sources, ascorbic acid, uric acid, coffeeberry formulation, fish oil providing polyunsaturated fatty acids, and Rhodiola Rosea, though evidence for benefits varies and is often inconclusive.

Yes, mutations in the glycyl-tRNA synthetase gene are associated with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 2D and, in some cases, distal spinal muscular atrophy type V, as well as other forms of the disease. Multiple reports detail disease-associated missense mutations and a novel mutation within this gene linked to CMT, and it is considered a causative gene for CMT2D and dSMA-V.

Yes, several software tools have been developed for automated analysis of FISH images, including novel software for accelerated evaluation of HER2 status, FISH Finder, and algorithms based on relocation and morphological spot counting. These tools aim to reduce the time and subjectivity associated with manual signal counting.

Histone methyltransferases post-translationally modify histones, regulating DNA accessibility and gene expression or silencing. These enzymes catalyze the addition of methyl groups to lysine residues in histone proteins, representing a stable epigenetic alteration. Specific histone methyltransferases, like G9a and GLP, can methylate lysine mimics, while others such as SUV39H1 establish histone H3 lysine 9 methylation which is connected with heterochromatinization. Furthermore, histone methylation can serve as a signal for the recruitment of histone acetyltransferases, like NuA3, to nucleosomes.

Yes, individuals with Dyskeratosis Congenita have an increased risk for cancer. The condition is described as a cancer predisposition syndrome, and patients exhibit increased susceptibility to various cancers including leukemia, colon carcinoma, osteogenic sarcoma, and female genital cancers. Additionally, mutations related to the syndrome can lead to cancer development, and the condition is linked to head and neck malignancies like squamous cell carcinoma.

Yes, miR-21 is deregulated in heart and vasculature under cardiovascular disease conditions such as proliferative vascular disease, cardiac hypertrophy and heart failure, and ischemic heart disease, and it is involved in the pathogenesis of these diseases. It contributes to the development of fibrosis and heart failure, and can contribute to myocardial disease by affecting cardiac fibroblasts. Targeting miR-21 has shown therapeutic efficacy in cardiovascular disease models.

BRCA1 possesses E3 ubiquitin ligase enzymatic activity, mediated by its N-terminal RING finger domain, though this activity is not essential for mammalian cell viability or homology-directed repair of double-strand DNA breaks.

Markers for autophagy include autophagy-related genes (ATGs), LC3-II, Beclin, Sirt1, Sirt3, FoxO1, FoxO3a, FoxO4, and the Bcl2/Bax ratio, with LC3C and NDP52 being important for antibacterial autophagy.

Yes, ChIPnorm is a two-stage statistical method proposed for normalizing ChIP-seq data and finding differential regions in the genome given two libraries of histone modifications from different cell types. Additionally, MMDiff is a method for detecting differences between sequence count data sets, quantifying shape changes in signal profiles.

Yes, a SNP at the 1q23 locus, predicted to disrupt transcription factor binding in CD84, was associated with response to etanercept therapy in rheumatoid arthritis patients. Additionally, CD84 gene expression correlated with disease activity, and genotypes and/or expression may serve as a biomarker for etanercept treatment in RA patients. Furthermore, CD84 molecules were identified as targets of autoantibodies in pemphigus vulgaris, an autoimmune disorder.

N-glycolylneuraminic acid can act as a decoy receptor to suppress infection of influenza A viruses that possess Neu5Gc binding ability, and it may play a significant role in human pathologies such as cancer and vascular disease.

Yes, andexanet alfa is a specific reversal agent against factor Xa inhibitors, including rivaroxaban, and aripazine reverses rivaroxaban as well. Clinical trials have demonstrated complete reversal of rivaroxaban’s anticoagulant activity with andexanet alfa within minutes.
Bicarbonate stimulates activation of AtxA for both toxin and capsule production, and elevated levels of CO2 enhance expression of the toxin genes in the presence of AtxA.

The provided text does not contain information about the function of 6SRNA in bacteria. However, the text details the methyl-directed mismatch repair system and the MutH protein’s role in recognizing and cleaving undermethylated GATC sequences during mismatch repair. This system, along with helicase II, impacts bacteriophage yield and the presence of GATC sequences in bacterial DNA. Therefore, while 6SRNA is not mentioned, the context focuses on DNA repair mechanisms and sequence-specific interactions within bacteria.

Yes, cytisine was found to be superior to nicotine-replacement therapy in helping smokers quit smoking when combined with brief behavioral support. At 1 month, continuous abstinence was reported for 40% of participants receiving cytisine compared to 31% receiving nicotine-replacement therapy, and this superiority continued at 1 week, 2 months, and 6 months.

Cysteine residues are frequently mutated in cases of CADASIL syndrome, with most mutations resulting in a gain or loss of cysteine, and approximately 95% of identified mutations being missense point mutations.

Mutations in the EZH2 gene are associated with Weaver syndrome, Sotos syndrome, and poor prognosis hematopoietic malignancies, and can also contribute to the development of myelodysplastic disorders including myelodysplastic syndromes and MDS/myeloproliferative neoplasm overlap disorders.

ExTopoDB and TMPDB are databases that contain experimentally derived topologies of α-helical transmembrane proteins, with TMPDB also including information on beta-stranded proteins and those with short pore-forming helices.
Hirschsprung disease is a congenital disorder characterized by the absence of intrinsic ganglion cells in the distal gastrointestinal tract.
Fanconi anemia is a disease characterized by hypersensitivity to DNA crosslinking agents and a high degree of chromosomal aberrations.
Oprozomib is orally bioavailable and is being investigated as an orally administered proteasome inhibitor.

Caveolae are plasma membrane invaginations, appearing as flask-shaped pits or vesicles, found in various tissues and cell types. They are enriched in cholesterol and sphingolipids and increase the plasma membrane surface area. These structures harbor signaling components like receptors and ion channels, and play roles in processes such as endocytosis, calcium signaling, and regulation of signaling events, acting as sensors, protectors, and organizers of the plasma membrane.

Chromatin compartments in the eukaryotic nucleus organize chromosomes, help establish heterochromatin, regulate RNA metabolism by controlling the availability of RNA processing proteins, constrain chromatin motion, and are involved in gene regulation, transcriptional programming, DNA damage repair, and the three-dimensional organization of chromatin.

No, abnormal dosage of ultraconserved elements is not disfavored in cancer cells; in fact, copy number variations arising specifically in cancer cells are typically not depleted for ultraconserved elements and can even become significantly enriched. This suggests a difference in how cancer cells handle these elements compared to healthy cells.

Yes, thyroid hormone regulates calcium transient in the myocardium. Several sources indicate thyroid hormone influences intracellular calcium transients, calcium flux, and the expression of genes encoding proteins involved in calcium handling such as SERCA2a, phospholamban, and the ryanodine channel. Hyperthyroidism was shown to reduce the time to peak calcium transient and the time to 80% decay of the peak calcium transient, demonstrating a direct effect on the calcium transient itself.

Proteins containing the SPRY domain are implicated in regulating innate and adaptive immunity, cytokine signaling, RNA metabolism, intracellular calcium release, immunity to retroviruses, regulatory and developmental processes, and modulating inflammasome activity to inhibit pro-IL-1beta processing, with some specifically recognizing and binding to viral capsids to block retroviral replication or sensing viral patterns.

Detecting gene fusion events across genomes can be used to predict functional associations of proteins, including physical interactions or complex formation. Selective pressure for certain genes to fuse over evolution suggests a functional relationship between the proteins involved. Analyzing these fusion events, particularly considering factors like domain composition and sequence similarity, helps elucidate protein function and identify associations within entire genomes. Furthermore, the frequent involvement of proteins with specific structural folds, like the alpha/beta fold, in fusion events provides insights into these functional connections.
NONO forms heterodimers with PSPC1, SFPQ (PSF), and potentially with E2A proteins as part of the Twist1 complex.
Mutant DVL1 is associated with Robinow syndrome and a specific subtype called RS-OS, as well as potentially contributing to prostate cancer progression.

Yes, proteasome inhibitors are good candidates for treatment of both leukemia and solid tumors, though with varying degrees of success. They have demonstrated efficacy in hematologic malignancies like multiple myeloma and are being translated to acute leukemia treatment, showing promising clinical activity, particularly when combined with conventional chemotherapeutics. While bortezomib is remarkably effective against multiple myeloma, its effect on solid tumors has been less encouraging, and a key obstacle is dose-limiting adverse effects in normal tissues. However, several proteasome inhibitors are currently in clinical trials to treat both multiple myeloma and solid tumors, and some have shown improved anti-tumor activities when combined with other drugs.

Yes, CTF4 and CTF18 are both required for sister chromatid cohesion and exhibit genetic and physical ties to replication fork constituents. They are also both necessary for high-fidelity chromosome segregation and act in association with the replication fork to facilitate sister chromatid cohesion.

The Genomic Regions Enrichment of Annotations Tool (GREAT) analyzes the functional significance of cis-regulatory regions identified through DNA binding events across the genome. It improves upon previous methods by incorporating distal binding sites and controlling for false positives using a binomial test. GREAT integrates annotations from 20 ontologies and is available as a web application, aiding in the recovery of functions missed by gene-based tools and generating testable hypotheses. It can be applied to various functional datasets, including ChIP-seq, open chromatin, and comparative genomics sets.
Olaparib targets poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase, and is a poly(adenosine 5'-diphosphate) ribose polymerase inhibitor.

No. While mutations in the thyroid hormone receptor beta gene are consistently linked to resistance to thyroid hormone, one study investigates the role of thyroid hormone receptor alpha1 in the *manifestation* of resistance to thyroid hormone in mice already possessing a mutation in the TR beta gene, suggesting it plays a compensatory role rather than being a primary cause of the syndrome. The context does not indicate that mutations in the thyroid hormone receptor alpha1 *cause* resistance to thyroid hormone.

RhoA expression is frequently increased in bladder cancer tissues, and elevated RhoA/Rho-kinase signaling contributes to the development and metastasis of the disease. Ras-induced activation of RhoA is involved in the invasion and migration of bladder cancer cells, and inhibiting RhoA may offer new therapeutic interventions. Higher RhoA expression is more common in higher grade tumors, though altered RhoA status does not appear to correlate with bladder cancer death in one study.
Human Orai1 and Orai3 channels, SHMT1 and SHMT2, and AMPARs are subject to a dimer-to-tetramer transition, with G6PD also forming a tetramer through dimer-dimer contacts.
Canagliflozin’s mechanism of action involves inhibiting the sodium glucose co-transporter 2, which reduces renal glucose reabsorption and increases urinary glucose excretion.

Thyroid hormone metabolism is altered in many patients with advanced heart failure and serves as an independent predictor of mortality. Specifically, low triiodothyronine (T3) syndrome and decreased T3 levels are associated with progressive deterioration of cardiac function, myocardial remodeling, and a poor prognosis. These levels can also add prognostic power to conventional cardiac parameters, indicating their usefulness in predicting outcomes for those with heart failure. Further studies are underway to evaluate the safety and effects of thyroid hormone treatment in these patients.

Mutations in the phospholamban gene associated with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy include the -42 C>G mutation, the -77A-->G mutation in the promoter region, a L39X nonsense mutation, and a Leu39Ter truncation mutation, as well as variants identified in calcium-handling genes like phospholamban itself, calsequestrin 2, calreticulin 3, and sorcin.
Transcription-coupled repair specifically acts on lesions in the transcribed strand of expressed genes.
Abnormalities in chromosomes 3p, 6q, 8q23, 10, 12p12, and 17q have been linked to Moyamoya disease.
The branch site consensus sequence in U12-dependent intron F is UUCCUUAAC.

The provided text does not contain information about Protein A from *Staphylococcus aureus* or its uses in biochemistry. The documents focus on identifying, screening for, and treating infections caused by *Staphylococcus aureus*, including methicillin-resistant strains, and describe antibacterial agents used against it. There is no mention of Protein A or its biochemical applications within these texts.

Clarithromycin and azithromycin represent a turning point in treating Mycobacterium avium infections, demonstrating high efficiency in both AIDS and non-AIDS patients. While clarithromycin can eliminate bacteremia, relapse is common due to pre-existing resistant mutants; resistance is linked to mutations in the 23S rRNA gene.  Additionally, a novel approach involves delivering the lytic phage TM4 via *Mycobacterium smegmatis* to kill intracellular *M. avium*, though some bacteria may develop resistance to the phage.

Treatment for acute pericarditis includes appropriate antibiotic therapy and surgical drainage, particularly in cases of purulent pericarditis. Inhibitors of the NLRP3 inflammasome, such as colchicine, and IL-1-targeted therapies can effectively blunt acute inflammation and reduce the risk of recurrences. In some cases, procedures like subxiphoidal tube drainage, intrapericardial thrombolysis, or even partial pericardiectomy may be necessary.
Tuberous sclerosis is a genetically heterogeneous autosomal dominant disease caused by mutations in either the TSC1 gene on chromosome 9 or the TSC2 gene on chromosome 16.

Chd1 is an ATP-dependent chromatin remodeling factor that regulates gene expression, incorporates H3.3 into chromatin, and functions in nucleosome assembly and positioning as well as histone turnover.
The FEC-75 regimen includes fluorouracil, epirubicin at a dosage of 75 mg/m2, and cyclophosphamide.
The recurrent translocation breakpoint on chromosome 22 of neuroepithelioma lies between the probes D22S1 and D22S15, and also between the loci VIIIF2 and LIF.

No. While triiodothyronine may improve some hemodynamic parameters like cardiac index and lower inotropic requirements, and can attenuate the postoperative decline in T3 concentrations, several studies demonstrate no significant effect on operative outcome, mortality, morbidity, arrhythmia, or the need for inotropic support, and routine use is not recommended. One study even showed increased mortality among women on thyroxine replacement therapy undergoing the procedure.

Computational methods for identifying cis-regulatory modules include statistical thin-tail tests, graph-based methods like MOPAT, algorithms that utilize ChIP datasets such as DePCRM, approaches assessing evolutionary conservation, and those employing search strategies like the ModuleSearcher and ModuleScanner algorithms to find combinations of transcription factor binding sites.
MLN4924 inhibits the NEDD8-activating enzyme (NAE), which initiates a protein homeostatic pathway essential for cancer cell growth and survival.
HS-1 associated protein X-1 (HAX-1) interacts with phospholamban and possesses anti-apoptotic function.

Yes, long QT syndrome is identified as a cause of sudden cardiac death in athletes, stemming from cardiac ion channel mutations that predispose individuals to this outcome, particularly during exercise. It is listed among the etiologies of sudden cardiac death and is noted as a growing area of understanding in diagnostic criteria.

MammaPrint is a prognostic profile used to assess the risk of distant recurrence and survival in breast cancer patients. It has been shown to accurately identify patients at low risk of developing metastases, with some studies demonstrating 100% of low-risk individuals remaining metastasis-free for up to five years. However, evidence regarding its clinical utility and added value to standard risk stratification is inadequate, and it is unclear which patient population would benefit most from the test.

Yes, M3/6 is described multiple times as a dual-specificity phosphatase, selective for JNK and also possessing specificity towards p38 MAP kinases. It is identified as one of the dual-specificity protein phosphatases and belongs to the CL100/MKP-1 family of dual-specificity phosphatases.

Yes, databases useful for proteomic analysis of human kidney tissue and urine exist and integrating these with general databases may aid research in nephrology, potentially revealing proteins involved in renal disease development or progression, and identifying biomarker candidates. Additionally, research focuses on key renal miRNAs and their role in pathomechanisms of renal diseases, suggesting data is being collected and analyzed in this area.

A numbering system was initially developed to determine the combining site of antibodies based on amino acid sequences, and this became known as the Kabat numbering system. This system was used to align Bence Jones proteins and calculate variability at each position, identifying complementarity determining regions. The Kabat database has expanded over time to include sequences for T cell receptors and MHC molecules, and it is used to derive structural and functional information from primary sequences. Revisions to sequence alignment and numbering for CH1 domains have also been made to be consistent with three-dimensional alignments.

Yes, DNA methyltransferase genes from both Arabidopsis and maize show similarity to mammalian de novo DNA methyltransferases of the Dnmt3 family, suggesting they encode plant de novo methyltransferases. Additionally, DRM2 and DRM3 are described as Arabidopsis thaliana Dnmt3 cytosine methyltransferase orthologs.
Current catalogs list approximately 24,500 putative protein-coding genes, though analysis suggests this number should be revised to around 20,500 genes.

No, vitamin D supplementation does not appear to reduce falls overall, though it may be effective in people who have lower vitamin D levels before treatment. Clinical trials have shown vitamin D did not reduce the rate or risk of falls, with rate ratios of 1.00 and risk ratios of 0.96 showing no statistically significant reduction.

Prophylactic antibiotic use, specifically azithromycin, may reduce the number of acute infectious episodes and hospital admissions in patients with severe, advanced COPD, and may also reduce the number of exacerbations though further study is needed to confirm this benefit.

Yes, frailty has been shown to be a predictor of depression, and depression is a major cause of poor nutritional status which is linked to frailty. Additionally, prospective associations were found between frailty and depression in a Chinese population, suggesting a significant role of frailty as a predictor of depression.
The provided text does not contain information about the generic name of Gliolan.

Yes, miR-155 suppresses the inhibitory effects of Jarid2 in Th17 cells. Specifically, miR-155 regulates the DNA-binding protein Jarid2 to relieve polycomb-mediated repression, and activation-induced miR-155 targets Jarid2 to regulate proinflammatory cytokine production in these cells. Defects in Th17 cell cytokine expression can be partially suppressed by Jarid2 deletion, further demonstrating a relationship.

enChIP is a novel method for purifying specific genomic regions while retaining their molecular interactions, enabling biochemical analysis. It utilizes engineered DNA-binding molecules—such as CRISPR or TAL proteins—to immunoprecipitate genomic regions, often with a tag and antibody, for identification of associated molecules. This technology, combined with mass spectrometry (enChIP-MS), can identify proteins linked to specific genomic loci and is useful for dissecting chromatin structure and analyzing genome functions like transcription and epigenetic regulation. Different systems, including retroviral expression systems, have been developed to enhance enChIP efficiency and minimize off-target effects.
The human HOXD cluster contains nine genes, including HOXD1, HOXD3, HOXD4, and HOXD8 through HOXD13, positioned sequentially from 3' to 5'.

No. Isotretinoin has severe teratogenic effects, potentially causing serious malformations of the craniofacial, cardiovascular, thymic, and central nervous systems in approximately 25-30% of babies exposed during the first trimester. Even after stopping the drug, there is a supposed possibility of foetal malformations after a long-term washout period. Pregnancy should be avoided during treatment and until circulating levels return to normal.
MDM2 is the E3 ubiquitin ligase that targets the tumor suppressor p53 for proteasomal degradation.

Yes, some DNA intercalators are inhibitors of DNA topoisomerase II, and may also act on DNA topoisomerase I by stabilizing cleavage intermediates. Additionally, most DNA intercalators and epipodophyllotoxins inhibit mammalian topoisomerase II by trapping the enzyme within DNA cleavage complexes.

MicroRNA-132 is implicated in progressive supranuclear palsy, schizophrenia, bronchopneumonia, and potentially neurodegenerative diseases generally, as well as conditions involving tauopathies and neuronal malfunction.

Human glutamate transporters include hEAAT2, EAAT1, EAAT2, EAAT3, EAAT4, and EAAT5, with GLT1 and EAAC1 also present in sperm alongside ionotropic glutamate receptors such as NR1, NR2B, GluR6, and KA2.

Sorting nexin 27 participates in vesicular and protein trafficking, specifically binding to early/recycling endosomes via its PX domain and interacting with proteins through its PDZ domain. It regulates the trafficking of potassium channels, leading to their endosomal movement and downregulation, and also influences the endocytosis and sorting of proteins like diacylglycerol kinase ζ and NMDA receptor 2C. Furthermore, SNX27 plays a role in T cell immunological synapse formation and can impact ERK phosphorylation when its expression is altered.

Yes, both orphan and gene-related CpG Islands follow power-law-like distributions in several genomes, as evidenced by linearity found in double logarithmic scale. This pattern is observed for both whole CGI populations and CGI subsets located away from known genes.
The proportion of non-canonical splice sites in the human genome is approximately 3.73% of GenBank annotated splice pairs, with 3.6% of 3' splice sites and 1.4% of 5' splice sites being inexact.

Coomassie brilliant blue, colloidal Coomassie blue, fluorescent dyes such as SYPRO Ruby, and modified silver staining are protein gel staining methods capable of visualizing the entire protein set, with colloidal Coomassie blue and a ‘blue silver’ implementation of colloidal Coomassie brilliant blue offering particularly high sensitivity and improved spot detection.

Aptamers are being investigated for clinical use as alternatives to antibodies, offering high specificity and being non-immunogenic. They have been developed as inhibitors for targets like vascular endothelial growth factor and thrombin, and the first aptamer-based therapeutic was FDA approved in 2004 for age-related macular degeneration. Furthermore, aptamers are being evaluated in clinical trials for diseases such as cancer, HIV, and are showing promise in targeted therapy, imaging, and nanotechnology applications.
The fungus Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense is the causative agent of Panama disease affecting bananas.

Nalmefene functions as an opioid antagonist, meaning it blocks the effects of narcotics. It can decrease repetitive behaviors, such as crib-biting in horses, potentially by impacting narcotic and dopamine receptors in the central nervous system. Additionally, nalmefene stimulates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, leading to increases in ACTH and cortisol levels.
The Mercedes Benz syndrome is characterized by synostosis of the sagittal and bilateral lambdoid sutures, and in some cases, additional sutural involvement may occur.

Yes, valproic acid is demonstrated to be a novel activator of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) in both mouse and human hepatocytes, and this activation leads to decreased liver mass, hepatic fat accumulation, and serum glucose in obese mice. Additionally, sorafenib, in combination with valproic acid, activates the AMPK pathway and exhibits synergistic antiproliferative effects.

Hedgehog, Notch, Wnt, c-Met, erbB2, IGF-R, TrkC, Myc, Sonic hedgehog/Patched, phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K), insulin-like growth factor (IGF), MAPK, and epidermal growth factor (EGF) signaling pathways have all been associated with medulloblastoma formation and growth.

Invadopodia are actin-based structures that play a role in cell adhesion, migration, and invasion, and they replace podosome-like structures during epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT). They are specialized membrane protrusions that degrade the extracellular matrix and are increasingly recognized as important drivers of local invasion in metastasis. Specifically, Twist1 promotes invadopodia formation via upregulation of platelet-derived growth factor receptor expression and activity, and induction of invadopodia is essential for Twist1 to promote tumor metastasis.

Cancer driver genes are genes that acquire genetic alterations and contribute to tumor development, enabling cancer hallmarks like genome instability and immune evasion. These genes are often mutated in a tissue-dependent manner, meaning they are altered in some cancers but not others, and can impact therapy response. A growing number of these genes are being identified through genomic analyses and databases, with some showing preference for mutations in specific tumor types, and methods are being developed to identify even rare driver mutations. Identifying these genes helps to understand tumorigenesis and may lead to improved therapeutic outcomes.

A mitochondrial nucleoid is a macromolecular assembly composed of one or more copies of mitochondrial DNA and associated proteins. These structures package mitochondrial DNA, ensuring its distribution and propagation within the cell, and serve as the molecular organizing unit of mitochondrial genetics. Nucleoids are also the site of interactions that determine the bioenergetic state of the cell and integrate mitochondrial DNA into cellular homeostasis. They range in size, with an average diameter of approximately 100nm, and a typical mitochondrion contains hundreds to thousands of them.

Treatment for amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis includes high doses of anti-thyroid drugs and steroids, either alone or in combination. Propylthiouracil (PTU) and dexamethasone have also been successfully used, and in refractory cases, lithium carbonate may be added to the treatment regimen. While thionamide, lithium, or radioactive iodine are generally ineffective for this specific condition, the disease course is typically benign and responds well to anti-thyroid medications with or without corticosteroids.

The cardiovascular response to thyroid hormone is similar to that of exercise, associating with increased left ventricular work. Increased thyroid hormone action increases the force and speed of systolic contraction and the speed of diastolic relaxation, which are largely beneficial effects. However, in the failing heart, decreases of thyroid hormone receptor levels occur, potentially contributing to diminished cardiac contractile function. While exercise mimics some effects of thyroid hormone, heart failure—which can limit exercise capacity—is linked to reduced thyroid hormone receptor expression.

Yes, the TCE is involved in spermatogenesis as it is necessary for translational control of expression in the male germ line of Drosophila melanogaster and is conserved among genes encoding structural proteins of the sperm tail. It also directs testis-specific transcription of genes.

Abacavir hypersensitivity is characterized by symptoms such as fever, skin rash, gastrointestinal disorders including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, and occasionally respiratory symptoms, though it can also manifest as enanthema without skin rash, and is strongly associated with a number of gastrointestinal symptoms.

Ivabradine treatment demonstrates improvement in exercise capacity and peak oxygen uptake in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. It also reduces the exercise-induced increase in the ratio of peak early diastolic mitral flow velocity to peak early diastolic mitral annular velocity. In a mouse model of the condition, ivabradine reduces aortic stiffness and fibrosis, and lowers left ventricular end-systolic elastance.

Yes, low T3 syndrome appears to be a prognostic marker in patients with renal insufficiency. One study found that alterations in thyroid function tests, commonly presenting as low T3 syndrome, were present in over 80% of patients with acute kidney injury (AKI), though these alterations did not appear to have clinical or prognostic implications in those patients. Additionally, a study involving patients with chronic renal insufficiency showed that low serum T3 concentrations were found in 43% to 54% of the group, and while these measurements did not accurately reflect metabolic state, low-T3 syndrome is considered a predictor of mortality in heart failure patients.

Yes, burning mouth syndrome appears to affect women, especially after menopause, more frequently than men. Several instances note the condition is particularly common in post-menopausal women, and women are diagnosed with symptoms seven times more frequently than males.
